coredata.dc:title: Near infrared-response ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the sensitive detection of Cu2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Min
Liu, Enzhou
Hu, Xiaoyun
Hao, Hong
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: The design of novel Cu2+ quantitative sensors with highly sensitivity and selectivity is a hot topic of modern chemistry. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence (PL) sensor based on the red-emitting CdTeS quantum dots (QDs) and blue-emitting polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDs) was firstly designed for sensing Cu2+. In the presence of Cu2+, a good linear relationship between the ratios (F520/F690) of the two peak intensities and the concentrations of Cu2+ was observed in the range of 0.01–250 µM with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.993 and the detection limit as low as 8.07 nM. The ratiometric nanosensor was also used to successfully determine Cu2+ in plasma samples with satisfactory results obtained. Therefore, we hold the opinion that the developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs will provide technical support for the point-of-caretesting of Cu2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdTeS QDs
PEI-CDs
Ratiometric sensor
Cu2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs
Fluorescence determination of Cu2+ with CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs
Detection mechanism of the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs for Cu2+
Determination of Cu2+ in plasma sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images of CdTeS QDs (a), PEI-CDs (b) and CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs (c and d). Inset: the local enlarged drawing.

XRD spectra of PEI-CDs (a) and CdTeS QDs (b), FT-IR spectra of CdTeS QDs (blue) and PEI-CDs (red) (c) and Full-scale XPS spectra of PEI-CDs (d). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PLE spectra (a) and UV-Vis spectra (b) of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs, PL spectra of CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs (c) and PL intensity variable trend graph of CdTeS QDs, PEI-CDs and CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs at the maximum emission wavelength under 360 nm UV lamp irradiation with different radiation times (d).

The PL spectra of CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs with different concentrations of Cu2+ (a), the linear plot of F520/F690 and the concentration of Cu2+ (b, c) and selectivity of the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs toward Cu2+ (d).
The effect of response time and pH on the response signal.
The UV–vis spectra of Cu2+, CdTeS QDs and CdTeS QDs+Cu2+ (a) and the fluorescence lifetime decays curves of CdTeS QDs in the presence and absence of Cu2+ (b).
Schematic illustration of the sensing mechanism for the detection of Cu2+ based on ratiometric fluorescent senor.
Determination results of Cu2+ in plasma samples.
Comparison of the performance of different analytical method for the detection of Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Near infrared-response ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the sensitive detection of Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of novel Cu2+ quantitative sensors with highly sensitivity and selectivity is a hot topic of modern chemistry. Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescence (PL) sensor based on the red-emitting CdTeS quantum dots (QDs) and blue-emitting polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDs) was firstly designed for sensing Cu2+. In the presence of Cu2+, a good linear relationship between the ratios (F520/F690) of the two peak intensities and the concentrations of Cu2+ was observed in the range of 0.01–250 µM with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.993 and the detection limit as low as 8.07 nM. The ratiometric nanosensor was also used to successfully determine Cu2+ in plasma samples with satisfactory results obtained. Therefore, we hold the opinion that the developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs will provide technical support for the point-of-caretesting of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions have posed a serious threat to the environment and even human health due to their high toxicity, carcinogenic effect and the ability to accumulate in the ecosystem and human body [1–3]. In particular, the abnormal concentration level of Cu2+, as one of the essential trace elements for human growth and development, is associated with many diseases, such as gastrointestinal diseases, Alzheimer's disease, liver or kidney damage, etc. [4–6]. In addition, the increasing use of Cu2+ in manufacturing, pharmaceutical and chemical industries has a continuous negative impact on the natural environment, which leads to Cu2+ becoming one of the heavy metal pollutants [7,8]. Currently, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency has clearly stipulated that the maximum allowable content of Cu2+ in drinking water is 1.3 mg/L [9]. Therefore, in order to monitor the occurrence of Cu2+ related diseases and the environmental pollution caused by Cu2+, it is urgent to develop a rapid, sensitive and selective method for sensing Cu2+ in environmental and biological samples. Nowadays, the progress of nanotechnology promotes the development of Cu2+ detection methods, including electrochemical, UV–vis spectrophotometry, electrochemiluminescence, colorimetric, atomic absorption spectrometry and fluorescence sensing technology [10–12]. Among the methods that have been reported, fluorescence analysis has attracted much attention due to its high sensitivity, simple and rapid operation [13,14]. Novel Cu2+ selective sensors have emerged in the literature, but the design and synthesis of high sensitivity and selective fluorescent biosensors for Cu2+ are still a challenge.

In recent years, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) are considered as a good candidate for designing fluorescent sensors due to their strong photobleaching resistance, high fluorescence quantum yield and tunable fluorescence properties [15–17]. Quantum dots have been widely used in chemical and biological sensors, but most of them are single signal modulation type, which are usually susceptible to various factors such as sensor inhomogeneous distribution and concentration, measurement condition and environment of the samples [18,19]. With the development of sensing technology, the new generation ratiometric fluorescence sensors using the intensity ratio of two distinct emission wavelengths as the detection signal have been introduced and gradually attracted the interest of researchers due to its multifaceted advantages, including self-calibration and precise quantification [20–22]. For instance, Mao’s group reported a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the detection of protamine and trypsin using Rox-DNA functionalized CdZnTeS QDs as fluorophore [23]. A dual-emission ratiometric sensor for sensing nitrite was prepared by Xiang’s group based on carbon dots (CDs) and Rhodamine B (RhB) assembled silica nanoparticles [24].

In the present study, the red-emitting CdTeS QDs and blue-emitting polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots (PEI-CDs) were used to construct the ratiometric sensor for sensing Cu2+ in plasma. It was noteworthy that CdTeS QDs (−29.1 ± 3.75 mV) and PEI-CDs (+33.5 ± 7.44 mV) have opposite charges, so there was electrostatic attraction between them which promoted the establishment of the ratiometric nanosensor. The constructed ratiometric sensor possessed two separated emission peaks at 520 nm and 690 nm under the excitation of 465 nm wavelength, which were used as reference and response signal, respectively. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence emission of CdTeS QDs was effectively suppressed at 690 nm, while the emission intensity of PE-CDs at 520 nm remained constant. A linear relationship between the PL intensity ratios and the concentrations of Cu2+ was observed in the range of 0.01–250 µM under the optimal experimental conditions. Finally, the applicability of the developed sensor in plasma samples has also been demonstrated and satisfactory results were obtained. We envisage that this work will provide an effective route for developing QDs-based ratiometric nanosensors for versatile applications in the quantitative analytical field.

3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) and Tellurium powder (Te) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Company. Cadmium chloride dehydrate (CdCl2.2H2O) and Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were provided from Tianjin Beilian Fine Chemicals Development Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was provided from Tianjin Ruijin special Chemical Co., Ltd. Thiourea was purchased from Tianjin Yongsheng Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Polyethyleneimine (PEI, 20000) was purchased from Xiya Reagents. Cupric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2.2H2O) was purchased from Tianli SciTech Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA) was purchase from Tianjin KGM Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents used in the experiments were analytical grade and used directly without further depuration.

Fluorescence performances were performed using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer Record (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a quartz cell (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm) and a 150 W Xenon light source. The Zeta potentials were measured using a Nano-ZS analyzer (Brookhaven Instruments Ltd, US). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were characterized by a Rigaku D/MAX 3C powder diffract meter. The size and structure of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs were obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (FEI Tecnai F20). UV-Vis absorption spectra were taken by a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) absorption spectra were carried out by Perkin Elmer Frontier FT-IR spectrometer in the mid IR range with ATR accessory. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained using Kratos AXIS NOVA spectrometer.

CdTeS QDs were synthesized as described in previous works [25]. The synthesis diagram was given in Supplementary Materials (Fig. S1). First, Te precursor solution was obtained by reducing Te powder (0.125 g) with sodium borohydride (0.113 g) under the protection of Ar atmosphere. At the same time, 0.228 g of CdCl2 and 177 μL of MPA were mixed in 50 mL of ultrapure water to obtain Cd precursor solution. Subsequently, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to about 9 using 1 M NaOH solution. Under the protection of Ar, the Te precursor solution and thiourea were injected sequentially to make CdTeS QDs nucleate rapidly. Finally, the resulting mixture was placed at 180 °C for 40 min, and cooled naturally to room temperature to obtain CdTeS QDs.

The preparation of water-soluble PEI-CDs was achieved through one-step hydrothermal raw materials according to a referencing method [26]. Briefly, 10 mL of mixture containing 0.5 g PEI and 1.0 g CA was heated for 6 h at 200 ℃. After the sample was naturally cooled to room temperature, the as-obtained PEI-CDs were filtrated using 0.22 µm water-based filter membrane to removed large particles and dialyzed against 150 mL ultrapure water for 15 h to remove unreacted impurities. The CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs nanocomposites were obtained by mechanically mixing CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs in different proportions.

The fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs were calculated based on the following equation [27,28]:
(1)
Q
x
=
Q
r
(
n
x
2
n
r
2
)
(
A
r
A
x
)
(
F
x
F
r
)

Here, x and r refer the determined and standard (Quinine sulfate, 0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, QY=0.54), respectively. Q, F, A and n on behalf of quantum yield, fluorescence peak area, absorption value at the excitation wavelength and refractive index of the solvent, respectively.

A certain concentration of Cu2+ aqueous solutions were freshly prepared before detecting. The following procedure for determining Cu2+ was accepted: To a 10 mL standard cuvette, 3 mL (0.02 M) CdTeS QDs, 0.5 mL (0.03 M) PEI-CDs, 1 mL (0.1 M, pH = 7.17) phosphate buffer and 1 mL Cu2+ with different concentration were added and the volume was calibrated to the scale line by ultrapure water. After incubation for 10 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were immediately measured and the ratios of fluorescence intensity at 520 and 690 nm (F520/F690) were calculated.

The plasma samples were collected from the ophthalmic vein of the mouse. Typically, the obtained blood was placed into eppendorf tubes with sodium heparin and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min (4 ℃) to get plasma. The obtained serum samples were diluted to 100-fold for use. The concentration of Cu2+ in serum samples was determined based on the standard addition method. Before the test, the standard Cu2+ solutions with concentration of 30, 50 and 70 μM respectively, was added to the serum samples to be tested. The next measurement procedure of Cu2+ was the same as that shown in 2.5.

The morphological structure of CdTeS QDs, PEI-CDs and CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. From the Fig. 1a and b, the as-fabricated CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs were almost spherical and homogenously dispersed with diameter 4.05 ± 0.49 and 11.61 ± 1.92 nm, respectively. The HRTEM (Insets) clearly exhibited the lattice fringes with interplanar d-spacing of 0.31 ± 0.01 and 0.39 ± 0.02 nm, indexing to the (111) plane of CdTeS QDs and (120) plane of PEI-CDs, respectively. Fig. 1c and d demonstrated TEM and HRTEM images of CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs. It could be observed that the existence of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs in the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs composite system, indicating the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs composite-sensor was successfully constructed. Additionally, the Zeta potential analysis (Fig. S2) of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs displayed that there was an electrostatic interaction with opposite charges between CdTeS QDs (−29.1 ± 3.75 mV) and PEI-CDs (+33.5 ± 7.44 mV).

XRD was used to investigate the crystalline structure and purity of the as-prepared CdTeS QDs, PEI-CDs and CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs nanocomposite. Fig. 2a showed the XRD pattern of PEI-CDs, it could be seen that an obviously broad peak was located at about 23°, corresponding to the (120) plane of the highly disordered C atoms (JCPDS No. 50-0926). Moreover, the XRD picture of CdTeS QDs (Fig. 2b) exhibited three different characteristic peaks at 26.29°, 43.49°, 51.39°, which are assigned to (111), (220) and (311) planes of the zinc blende structure. And, there were no other impurity peaks, indicating that no phase separation occurred during the synthesis process. In the XRD spectrum of the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs nanocomposite, the diffraction peaks of CdTeS QDs could be clearly observed, but the diffraction peaks of PEI-CDs did not appear, which was because the concentration of PEI-CDs in the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs nanocomposite was low. In addition, compared with the diffraction peaks of pure CdTeS QDs, all three diffraction peaks moved in a large angle direction in the nanocomposite, which was the result of the interaction between CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs.

The results of FT-IR absorption spectra analysis of CdTeS QDs (red curve), PEI-CDs (blue curve) and CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs nanocomposite (black curve) were showed in Fig. 2c. There were three obviously characteristic peaks at about 3400, 1400, and 1560 cm–1 in the FT-IR spectrum of CdTeS QDs, which are ascribed to the vibration peak of ‒OH group, symmetric and anti-symmetry stretching peaks of ‒COOH group, respectively. The broad peaks around 3474 and 1637 cm–1 of PEI-CDs were assigned to the N‒H stretching and bending vibration modes, respectively. The characteristic peak at 1549 cm−1 corresponded to the C˭ONR bond, indicating that PEI can interact with the carboxyl groups of the carbon dots through amidation reaction to achieve surface modification and provide positive charge for carbon dots [29]. After CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs were combined, the characteristic vibration peaks of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs appeared in the FT-IR spectrum of CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs nanocomposite, which indicates that the two were successfully combined together.

XPS analysis was used to characterize the chemical state of the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs. Fig. 2d revealed the full-rang spectra with characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Cd 3d, Te 3d and S 2p [30,31]. The C1s peak could be deconvoluted into two peaks relating to C˭O/C˭N (284.8 eV) and C˭C (288.2 eV) [32] (Fig. S3a). The presence of C˭C was seen as the main constituent part of the PEI-CDs skeleton and the existence of C˭O structure was bounded up with baring hydroxyl or C‒O‒C, which illustrating intermolecular dehydration occurred during the synthesis of PEI-CDs [29]. In Fig. S3b, the binding energy peaks at 400.0 eV corresponding to the C‒N‒C. The three peaks located at 531.3, 532.7 and 535.6 eV were attributed to the C˭O, C‒OH/C‒O‒C and C‒O, respectively (Fig. S3c) [33]. Moreover, the Cd3d peak presented in Fig. S3d could be split into two peaks at 405.2 eV (Cd 3d5/2) and 411.9 eV (Cd 3d3/2). The deconvolution of the high-resolution Te 3d XPS spectrum (Fig. S3e) revealed two peaks at 572.4 and 582.8 eV, corresponding to Te 3d5/2 and Te 3d3/2 respectively. The S 2p peak ( Fig. 3f) could be divided into two peaks at 161.9 and 163.6 eV, confirming the presence of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 [34]. These results illustrated the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs was successfully combined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs in this work were prepared by aqueous synthesis and hydrothermal methods, respectively. And the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs nanocomposites were constructed based on the electrostatic attraction between CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs. The fluorescence QYs of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs were calculated as 33.73% and 22.88%, respectively, using quinine sulfate as reference. The optical performances of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs were studied by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy.

As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence excitation spectra (PLE) of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs had a wide overlap, which facilitating the simultaneous excitation of both materials at the same excitation wavelength. The prepared CdTeS QDs (Fig. 3b blue carve) displayed a wide adsorption band of ultraviolet and visible light. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of PEI-CDs (Fig. 3b red carve) exhibited a sharp peak at 356 nm, which was belonged to n→п* transition of C˭O [35]. Fig. 3c showed the fluorescence emission spectra of CdTeS QDs (blue), PEI-CDs (red) and CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs (black). Under the same excitation, CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs exhibited well resolved dual emission peaks at 690 and 520 nm, corresponding to the emission of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs, respectively. In order to further emphasize the modification effect of PEI, the fluorescence properties of CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs and CdTeS QDs/CDs (Fig. S4) were compared. There was only one emission peak of CdTeS QDs in the CdTeS QDs/CDs system, which could be due to the lack of functional groups that can bind CdTeS QDs on the surface of CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity values of CdTeS QDs, PEI-CDs and CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs were recorded after UV lamp irradiation for different time, in order to study the photo-stability of the as-prepared materials. It could be demonstrated from Fig. 3d that the fluorescence intensity values of F690, F520 and F520/F690 were almost unchanged with the extension of radiation time (F690 and F520 were the fluorescence intensity values of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs at the maximum emission wavelength, respectively. F520/F690 was the ratio of fluorescence intensity of two emission wavelengths in the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs composite system), which indicated that CdTeS QDs, PEI-CDs and CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs have strong light resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of Cu2+ with CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The incorporated of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs can realize the ratiometric detection of Cu2+, which has the advantages of self-calibration and precise quantification compared with single-wavelength modulated fluorescent probes. As mentioned above, the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs nanocomposite system was successfully constructed by the electrostatic attraction between CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs. From the Fig. 4a, the fluorescence emission of CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs at 690 nm was gradually quenched while the emission at 520 nm remained constant in the presence of Cu2+, and the ratios of the fluorescence intensity rose (F520/F690) with the increase of Cu2+ concentration (Fig. 4b). There was a good linear relationship (y = 0.755 +0.0014x, y and x represented the F520/F690 ratio values and Cu2+ concentration, respectively) between F520/F690 and the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0.01–250 μM with the correlation coefficient 0.993 (Fig. 4c). The detection limit was calculated as 8.07 nM based on the criterion of 3σ/k, where σ and k were the standard deviation of at least ten measurements of a blank signal and the slope of linear curve, respectively [36]. Furthermore, the detection performance of Cu2+ detection with CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs alone had been collected and were showed in Fig. S5. The emission intensity gradually declined as the Cu2+ concentration increases, when CdTeS QDs was used as a fluorescent sensor. However, compared with the ratiometric fluorescence sensor constructed in the work, its linear range was relatively narrow. The fluorescence emission of PEI-CDs did not fluctuate significantly with the change of Cu2+ concentration, which indicates that the quantification of Cu2+ cannot be achieved by using PEI-CDs as a fluorescence sensor.

To estimate the selectivity of Cu2+ assay method, the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensor was used to test some potential substances, such as Fe2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cl- and so on (the concentration of all interfering substances was 100 μM). Evidently, only Cu2+ revealed a noticeable increasement for the response signals (Fig. 4d). The above results illustrated that the proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs can achieve sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+.

To receive the best incubation time between the ratiometric fluorescence sensor and Cu2+, a series of experiments were conducted. The results of the study were showed in Fig. 5a, the reaction could reach equilibrium quickly upon adding Cu2+ after incubation for 1 min, implying that the as-prepared CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs can realize the rapid detection of Cu2+. Furthermore, the fluorescence signal F690/520 was relatively strong in acidic and weak acidic environment, but the changes were not obvious under neutral and alkaline conditions (Fig. 5b, black). To explain this phenomenon, the fluorescence intensities of CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs were recorded in different pH environments. The fluorescence emission of PEI-CDs at 520 nm (red) did not fluctuate significantly with the change of pH, while the emission intensity of CdTeS QDs at 690 nm was low under acidic conditions and stable under neutral and alkaline conditions. In view of the fact that the Cu(OH)2 precipitates were formed by Cu2+ under alkaline conditions [37], neutral conditions (pH = 7.17) were selected for the sensing of Cu2+ in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of the CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs for Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the CdTeS QDs fluorescence emission was effectively suppressed by the introduction of Cu2+. It is well known that energy transfer, electron transfer and the formation of non-fluorescent ground-state substances have been developed to explain the fluorescence quenching effect [38]. In the presented study, no variation was observed in the absorption spectrum of CdTeS QDs in the presence of Cu2+ and there was no appearance of any absorption band in the absorption spectrum of Cu2+ ( Fig. 6a), which excluding the fluorescence quenching induced by the formation of non-fluorescent ground-state substances. In addition, there was no overlap between the emission or excitation spectrum of CdTeS QDs and the absorption spectrum of Cu2+, which ruled out energy transfer between them. Therefore, electron transfer from CdTeS QDs to Cu2+ was the possible reason for fluorescence quenching, which was due to the unique d9 electron configuration of Cu2+, making Cu2+ easy to accept an electron to form a d10 full stable structure ( Scheme 1) [39], this could be validated by the changes in the average fluorescence lifetime. The average fluorescence lifetimes of CdTeS QDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ were 75.372 and 67.874 ns, respectively (Fig. 6b). The decrease in fluorescence lifetime proved that it was reasonable given that electron transfer between CdTeS QDs and Cu2+ was responsible for fluorescence quenching of CdTeS QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in plasma sample
ce:section.ce:para: Plasma samples were chosen to investigate the feasibility of as-designed ratiometric nanosensor for Cu2+ detection by standard addition method. The measured results were summarized in Table 1, the recoveries in plasma samples were close to 100% with low relative standard deviations (RSD), illustrating the good creditability of the newly constructed sensing method for the determination of Cu2+. Furthermore, the sensing performance of the as-prepared CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs was compared with the other reported sensors for detecting Cu2+ in Table 2, the linear range and detection limit (Fig. S6) of this ratiometric fluorescence sensor were comparable. These analysis results illustrate that our proposed CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs with a series of attractive superiority have promising application in the rapid, sensitive and selective determination of Cu2+.

In a word, we have successfully developed a novel CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensors with two separated emission peaks at 520 and 690 nm for the detection of Cu2+. The most significant advantage of the as-designed ratiometric nanosensor was that the CdTeS QDs and PEI-CDs could be effectively combined by electrostatic attraction to avoid complex chemical modification. In the presence of Cu2+, the electron transfer between CdTeS QDs and Cu2+ induced the fluorescence quenching of CdTeS QDs/PEI-CDs at 690 nm. The developed sensing strategy exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity for the quantification of Cu2+ and was used in plasma samples with satisfactory results.
Min Yang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Enzhou Liu: Funding acquisition. Xiaoyun Hu: Funding acquisition. Hong Hao: Validation. Jun Fan: Funding acquisition, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M600809).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.126812.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyamine-capped gold nanorod as a localized surface Plasmon resonance probe for rapid and sensitive copper(II) ion detection
J. Colloid Interface Sci.

Environ. Sci. Nano
